The topic of self-driving cars is highly debated especially in light of recent event with the death of an Arizona woman at the hands of a self-driving Uber. Those for self-driving cars argue that they reduce the rate of accidents as a result of distracted drivers, improve traffic flow, are cost saving in terms of time and monetary value from insurance, and could allow greater access to transportation for the disabled and elderly. Those against self-driving cars argue the system does not eliminate accidents, that there is a learning curve with the technology, that it would take away profit from other sectors of the economy, and that there are instances in which the technology is not equipped to make a decision. While both sides make valid points, the benefits of adopting a system of self-driving cars rises to the forefront. 

Many people are quick to point out the flaws in self-driving cars but it is still in its infancy and has the ability to develop and improve with time whereas we have not seen that in human motorists over the years. Certainly we may expect the number of accidents by self-driving cars to rise as it is more widely adopted but this number palls in comparison to the number of accidents caused by drivers. Tragedies such as those in Arizona should not happen but unfortunately with nearly 1.3 million people dying in motor vehicle accidents a year and 81 percent of accidents being the result of human error it appears the main culprit is us. As humans we already rely on computers and technology for many daily tasks and self-driving cars are an extension of the innovative mindset our tech fields have embraced. 

Those against also argue that investing in self-driving cars may take away money from other industries and sectors of the economy. Again while self-driving cars may impact the profits of other transportation system, overall the profits from developing and implementing this technology will outweigh the losses. There is a reason many people invest in tech companies as the have become more profitable in the 21st century. Especially with the start-up of new technology, as they industry grows this will demand that more and more jobs be created to replace the ones lost. Also, savings in terms of reduced accidents are astronomical. The U.S. Department of Transportation has assigned a value of 9.2 million for each human life, with savings coming from venues such as healthcare and insurance. There is also the argument that self-driving cars may also save you time as they free up your transportation time to attend to other daily tasks when you do not have to focus on the driving. With more efficient driving the self-driving cars have the potential to decrease driving time and traffic as well.

Though people are still resistant to the idea of self-driving cars it is already in ways being implemented into our driving with features such as autonomous braking, self-parking and sensor detectors. The technology for this field is currently available and calls on our investment to develop and improve. While no system is perfect, one that saves us lives and on costs should be highly considered.

output_text:
The advent of self-driving cars has ignited a fervent public debate, particularly in the wake of tragic incidents such as the death involving an autonomous Uber in Arizona. Proponents ardently argue that these vehicles promise a substantial reduction in accidents stemming from human distraction, a significant improvement in traffic flow, and considerable economic benefits through reduced insurance costs and reclaimed time. Moreover, they envision a future of enhanced mobility and accessibility for the elderly and disabled. Conversely, skeptics contend that autonomous systems, while advanced, do not eliminate accidents entirely, face a steep learning curve, pose a threat to existing economic sectors by displacing jobs, and may prove unreliable in complex, unpredictable decision-making scenarios. While both perspectives offer valid concerns and insights, the compelling advantages of integrating autonomous vehicles into our transportation infrastructure ultimately rise to the forefront, demanding serious consideration.

Critics are quick to highlight the imperfections of self-driving cars, yet this nascent technology possesses an inherent capacity for continuous learning and refinement—a stark contrast to the relatively static performance of human drivers over decades. While an increase in autonomous vehicle accidents may accompany wider adoption, these figures are projected to remain dwarfed by the staggering toll of human-caused collisions. Tragedies like the Arizona incident are deeply regrettable, but the broader reality is grim: nearly 1.3 million lives are lost annually in motor vehicle accidents, with human error implicated in 81 percent of these incidents. This data unequivocally points to human fallibility as the predominant factor. Given our increasing reliance on technology for myriad daily tasks, self-driving cars represent a logical and inevitable progression of human innovation.

Opponents also voice concerns that investment in autonomous vehicles could divert capital and diminish profits from other economic sectors. While certain existing transportation models may experience disruption, the overall economic gains from developing and deploying this advanced technology are poised to be substantial. The burgeoning profitability of 21st-century tech companies underscores the potential for growth. As this industry matures, it will inevitably generate a wealth of new jobs in research, development, manufacturing, and maintenance, offsetting potential losses in other areas. Moreover, the financial savings from accident reduction are immense. The U.S. Department of Transportation estimates the value of a human life at $9.2 million, implying colossal savings in healthcare costs, insurance payouts, and property damage. Beyond monetary gains, autonomous vehicles offer the invaluable benefit of reclaimed time, allowing occupants to engage in other tasks during commutes. Their inherent efficiency also promises to reduce overall driving times and alleviate traffic congestion.

Despite lingering public apprehension, elements of autonomous technology are already integrated into modern vehicles, manifesting in features like adaptive cruise control, autonomous emergency braking, and self-parking systems. The foundational technology is not only available but demands continued investment to reach its full potential. While no technological system can ever promise absolute perfection, one that offers the profound advantages of saving countless lives and delivering significant economic efficiencies warrants not just consideration, but proactive embrace.

draft1:
Self-driving car is a new technology and receives a lot of debates recently. On one hand, self-driving cars offer positive impacts, e.g. safer commuting, cost savings, and give opportunities to many people who can't travel by themselves at this moment. On the other hand, people also raise concerns about the security, safety, and economy of the technology.
In my opinion, I support bringing self-driving cars into reality. The technology can bring many benefits to humanity. First, we, as human in modern societies, will save a lot of money thanks to the technology. Because when a lot of self-driving cars are on the street and they can communicate with each other, they can reduce the problem with congestion or unknown traffic conditions. Second, self-driving cars are supposed to be more safety because it can eliminate mistakes made by a human. The most common mistake is driving under the influence (of alcohol, drugs, or tireness). Moreover, drivers tend to have false confidence as they have more driving hours. This false confidence will increase the chances that an accident will happen. Last but not least, self-driving cars put an equity for people having difficulties in traveling, such as old people or disabilities. While public transportations are available in all cities, in many cases, they can only take a person to a pre-designated location (stop) rather than the real destination. Public transportations are operated with a fixed schedule which does not perfectly match everyone's situations. 
The cons about self-driving cars could be categorized into two groups: not-ready and not-true. The not-ready group contains arguments about new challenges that the technology will bring in when it is deployed in mass. For example, when an accident is made by a self-driving car, who will hold the responsibility? Or security issues such as how can drivers protect their personal data and what will happen if the car is hijacked by a hi-tech criminal? Interestingly, what would the car-makers or software designers choose in moral-sensitive situations? For example, given an unavoidable accident will happen, which will either kill an old pedestrian crossing the street or a baby in a stroller on the pavement, which choice should be made by the self-driving car? These issues must be addressed or disclosed before deploying self-driving cars in the mass. The not-true group consists of false assumptions about the self-driving technology. For example, there are concerns about drivers are no longer equipped with the skills to operate cars. In fact, we are living well in a society without knowing much about our daily stuffs. I don't know anything about fixing a car, but I can always call AAA or my friends whenever my car breaking down. I use the washing machine at least twice a week without knowing how to make such a machine. Another false opinion is the difference between states regarding turning right on red or cars cannot interpret police officers' signals when directing traffics. In fact, the car can update its algorithm according to the state it is traveling simply based on GPS signal. Moreover, when all cars on the streets are self-driving cars, the police officer does not need to go down the street and directs the traffic but just sends information to the car through a communication channel, e.g. 3G or wifi. Last but not least, some people even afraid that auto manufacturers might sell fewer cars. This is not true as a self-driving car is still a car which needs a manufacturer. In fact, we have seen more and more car manufacturers join the self-driving race nowadays, such as Volvo, BMW, Toyota, Honda, and many more.
In conclusion, I think self-driving car is a revolution that will have more benefit to our society. However, there are still some concerns needed to be addressed in order to make this technology available to everyone.

output_text:
The advent of self-driving cars marks a significant technological advancement, sparking widespread debate regarding their potential impact. While autonomous vehicles promise numerous benefits, including enhanced safety, economic efficiencies, and increased accessibility, they also raise legitimate concerns about security, accountability, and their broader societal implications. In my view, the integration of self-driving cars into our daily lives is a desirable and achievable goal, given their immense potential to benefit humanity.

Firstly, modern societies stand to gain significant economic advantages from autonomous vehicles. By allowing cars to communicate and optimize routes, this technology can dramatically mitigate traffic congestion and dynamically respond to evolving road conditions, leading to substantial savings in time, fuel, and infrastructure costs for individuals and the economy as a whole. Secondly, self-driving cars are designed to be inherently safer by eliminating human error, which is responsible for the vast majority of accidents. This includes preventing incidents caused by impairment (e.g., alcohol, drugs, fatigue) or the overconfidence that often accompanies extensive driving experience. The precision and consistent adherence to traffic laws by autonomous systems could drastically reduce accident rates and save countless lives. Lastly, autonomous vehicles foster greater equity for individuals with mobility challenges, such as the elderly or those with disabilities. While public transportation is available, it often restricts travel to fixed stops rather than precise, door-to-door destinations and operates on schedules that may not align with everyone's needs. Self-driving cars offer personalized, on-demand transportation, granting unprecedented freedom and independence to those currently limited in their ability to travel.

Concerns surrounding autonomous vehicles can broadly be categorized into legitimate challenges requiring resolution and common misconceptions. The former group encompasses new complexities that demand clear frameworks before mass deployment. For instance, the question of legal liability in the event of an accident involving an autonomous vehicle remains a complex issue. Cybersecurity is another critical area; robust protections are needed against data breaches or potential hijacking by high-tech criminals. Furthermore, the ethical implications of programming autonomous vehicles to make decisions in unavoidable, moral-sensitive dilemmas—such as choosing between two harmful outcomes—require profound societal and philosophical deliberation. These critical issues demand comprehensive solutions and transparent frameworks from policymakers, manufacturers, and society at large.

Conversely, many anxieties are based on false assumptions about the technology. One common concern is the potential erosion of human driving skills. However, our reliance on complex technologies, from washing machines to smartphones, rarely necessitates a deep understanding of their internal workings or the ability to repair them; we simply use them effectively. Similarly, professional services would likely emerge to address maintenance and emergency needs for autonomous vehicles. Another misconception relates to the ability of cars to handle variations in traffic laws (e.g., right-on-red rules) or to interpret human police signals. In reality, autonomous systems can integrate real-time data, including GPS location, to adapt to local regulations. Furthermore, vehicle-to-infrastructure (V2I) communication could enable direct digital transmission of traffic instructions from authorities, obviating the need for manual signals. Finally, the fear that autonomous technology might lead to decreased car sales overlooks the fact that autonomous vehicles are still manufactured vehicles, simply with advanced capabilities. The growing investment by major automotive manufacturers (e.g., Volvo, BMW, Toyota, Honda) into autonomous research signals a transformation, not a decline, in the industry.

In conclusion, autonomous vehicles represent a revolutionary leap forward with the potential to profoundly benefit society. While significant ethical, legal, and operational challenges remain, they are surmountable. Their resolution will pave the way for a safer, more efficient, and inclusive future of transportation, making this transformative technology available and beneficial to everyone.

draft1:
Self-driving cars, though only recently becoming a technological feasibility, have advanced rapidly in their short time of development and have thoroughly captured the public eye. The debate on their fitness to our automotive environment and on their desirableness to our society as a whole is raging onward with impassioned argument on both sides. In this essay I will aim to provide convincing evidence that though it is not perfect, the prospect of driver-less roads would mark a definitive improvement over our current conditions. 

To start with, consider the fallibility of human drivers. On a regular basis we turn on our engines without sufficient sleep, absentmindedly forget to check blind-spots, and allow our attention to be torn from the road by the slightest of mobile vibrations. With an estimated 33,000 annual fatal accidents and overwhelming evidence that human error accounts for a significant number of these it seems incontrovertible that we should do better. Although it is not yet certain that self-driving cars in their current condition would cause that number to go down, the lack of human fallibility makes the prospect seem likely. Furthermore, whereas human driver tend to fall into a lull of false confidence when they have engaged in a task for an extended period of time, self-driving cars can be designed to improve and become more safe over time. There is no distraction, no sleep deprivation, no intoxication, and no frantic concern for being late to take a self-driving car away from the task of safely getting from point A to point B. 

Furthermore, self-driving cars could give society a method of transportation which is much less restrictive. Currently, driving privileges are limited to those capable of operating the machinery, passing their test, and -for the most part - affording to own their own vehicle. If a child needs to be transported to any sort of extracurricular event they are bound by their parents' schedules and mobilities. If a senior citizen who has been deemed unsafe to drive decides to go shopping they must wait for long periods to use public transportation with conditions that could be extremely painful or uncomfortable with those who experience chronic pain from arthritis or similar conditions. Furthermore, many who cannot afford their own car are forced to commute long distances to their jobs, health-care, and programs, forcing a de facto restriction on who may access what. Although it is likely that one who cannot afford a regular car could not afford their own self-driving car, there is nothing to say that they must have their own. The ability to have a car drive back to the place it left from could allow a much more intricate form of car sharing; additionally, the current restrictions on insured drivers that hassle any type of shared vehicle would likely be thoroughly relaxed or missing entirely for a driver-less car. Even beyond the prospect of shared cars, self-driving vehicles could revolutionize public transportation, making a wider range of pick up locations and drop off locations feasible. If large buses or a huge fleet of public cars were to have flexible routes that could give people who do not have the means to own their own car a transportational freedom akin to it at a fraction of the price that services like Uber and Lift offer today. 

A society which supports self-driving cars would certainly look different from ours. How we implement them will determine much of their success and failure. For that reason it is not a guaranteed success, however, looking at the massive limitations and problems with our current system, it does not seem to be a stretch to claim that this could bring us dramatic improvements.

Self-driving cars, though only recently having become a technological feasibility, have advanced rapidly in their short time of development, managing to thoroughly capture the public eye. A debate on their fitness to our automotive environment and on their desirableness to our society as a whole has been raging onward with impassioned arguments on both sides. In this essay I aim to provide convincing evidence that, though they have potential to cause societal harm, given the distressing array of negative societal impacts associated with human-driven cars, the apparent feasibility for self-driving cars to ameliorate these issues makes it only rational to welcome their implementation. 
	To start with, consider the fallibility of human drivers. On a regular basis we turn on our engines without sufficient sleep, absentmindedly forget to check blind-spots, and allow our attention to be torn from the road by the slightest of mobile vibrations. With an estimated 33,000 annual fatal accidents and overwhelming evidence that human error accounts for a significant number of these, it seems incontrovertible that we should do better. 
	While no one would rightly argue that our current system is ideal, skepticism toward self-driving cars at this stage is more than fair. The development of the vehicles is very much still in its developmental infancy. Coupling that fact with the reality of a mixed automotive environment - with both computerized and human driven cars - one might very well expect implementation of self-driving cars to cause an increase in accidents. The key question then becomes: "would a self-driving car actually be any safer than a human-driven one?". While there is currently insufficient information to give a definite answer, I think there are good reasons to put more faith in the self-driving cars. Whereas human drivers tend to fall into a lull of false confidence after driving for many years, self-driving cars can be designed to improve and become more safe over time. Further, there is no distraction, no sleep deprivation, no intoxication, and no frantic concern for being late to take a self-driving car away from the task of safely getting from point A to point B. In the absence of such risks, a computerized car might very well behave safer even without the ability to intuitively grasp tendencies and perceptions of neighboring human drivers. 
	Others however, are more concerned about the societal impact of these cars than their safety specs. Even if the change means less lives lost on the road, it still may be an unwise one if it leads to the ruin of lives off the road. Given the number of transportation jobs, a computerized revolution could displace the occupations of many currently employed drivers. Furthermore, given the massive economic investment needed to own such a car, might this lead to a widening of already egregious inequality gaps in the country? 
	The transportation industry represents a real threat if we have no place for the newly unemployed to turn. This however, is not a problem unique to self-driving cars but rather, is a pervasive and imminent threat encroaching on us from all directions given the rapid advance of technological competency. If we cannot, as a society solve this more generally, the threats will be realized even in the absence of the driver-less car revolution; if we do find a way to support the displaced workers of other sectors I remain hopeful that such approaches can be implemented to the transportation-sector as well. 
	As for societal inequality, self-driving vehicles, if implemented properly, could actually be a great boon to economically struggling classes. To begin, the previously considered transportation industry automation, though bad for workers, could be fantastic for commodity prices. If the cost of all daily goods dropped, many would be the better for it. Further, current systems of public transportation are woefully insufficient to serve their desired societal role. When jobs, hospitals, and enrichment events are inconvenient to access, attendance from under serviced individuals tends to drop. This creates a de-facto restriction on who may access what. With an automated public transportation system, it could be much easier to introduce flexible pick-up locations, drop-off spots, and schedules. With a more functional and convenient public transportation system, we could relieve both the economic burden of buying a vehicle as well as the logistical burden of navigating tricky and time-consuming bus routes. 
	A society which supports self-driving cars would certainly look different from ours. How we implement them will determine whether those differences were successes or failures. For that reason it is not a guaranteed success, however, looking at the massive limitations and problems with our current system, it is reasonable to maintain optimism about the ability of self-driving cars to bring about dramatic improvements to our lives.

output_text:
Self-driving cars, once a futuristic concept, have rapidly transitioned from technological feasibility to a prominent public discussion. The debate surrounding their integration into our automotive landscape and their overall societal desirability is intense. This essay will argue that despite inherent imperfections, the advent of autonomous vehicles promises a significant improvement over our current transportation paradigm.

The inherent fallibility of human drivers stands as a primary justification for autonomous vehicles. We routinely operate cars while fatigued, neglect crucial safety checks like blind-spot monitoring, and succumb to distractions from mobile devices. Given an estimated 33,000 annual fatal accidents, with human error overwhelmingly implicated in a significant portion, the imperative for improvement is clear. While the immediate impact of current self-driving technology on these statistics remains to be fully quantified, the elimination of human frailties – such as distraction, sleep deprivation, intoxication, and emotional urgency – strongly suggests a substantial reduction in accidents. Moreover, unlike human drivers who can develop complacency over time, autonomous systems are engineered for continuous learning and improvement, enhancing safety with every mile driven.

Beyond safety, autonomous vehicles promise a significant expansion of transportation access, liberating society from current restrictions. Presently, driving is contingent on physical capability, licensing, and often, vehicle ownership. This creates substantial barriers: children depend on parental schedules for activities, elderly individuals deemed unsafe to drive face long waits and discomfort on public transport, and those unable to afford a car endure arduous commutes, limiting access to employment, healthcare, and essential services. While individual ownership of an autonomous vehicle might remain prohibitive for some, the technology facilitates innovative car-sharing models. Vehicles could return to a central hub or circulate efficiently, drastically reducing the need for personal ownership. Furthermore, the complexities of insured drivers and shared vehicles would likely be simplified or rendered obsolete in a driverless paradigm. This potential extends to public transportation, where self-driving fleets could offer flexible, on-demand routes, providing transportation freedom akin to private ownership at a fraction of the cost of current ride-sharing services, thereby bridging critical accessibility gaps for countless individuals.

The integration of self-driving cars will undoubtedly reshape society, with their ultimate success heavily dependent on careful implementation. While challenges remain and guaranteed success is elusive, the profound limitations and systemic problems inherent in our current human-driven transportation system make a compelling case for the transformative and dramatically improved future promised by autonomous vehicles.

There are many advantages for the United States to transition to self-driving cars. The primary benefit is simply the amount of lives saved due to the elimination of intoxicated, distracted, or otherwise dangerous drivers. Computers provide a precise, safe alternative to the potential for human error that occurs each time a driver gets behind the wheel. In addition, self-driving cars can save money on insurance costs and outdated infrastructure, decrease traffic, and are actually better for the environment.  However, some limitations to self-driving cars exist. These include a difficult transition to using these vehicles, cost, the failure of technology, and the potential for job loss and decline of certain industries.
	Frequently, we hear arguments for or against something with the justification that it benefits or does not benefit society.  In order to make an argument for either one of these, it is imperative that we consider the following questions: who is society, what is good for society, what does society need, and at what expense to the individual do we support the public good?
	In this case, the answer to these questions is easy. Anything that has the potential to save human lives is both good for the individual and good for society. Despite the limitations in technology, an initial cost, and a likely challenging transition, self-driving cars are the future, and they will save lives. No other benefit matters. No other limitation should stop us.  
America has a serious, dangerous driving problem. Every year, 33,000 people die in automobile accidents, and most of these deaths should have been prevented because the drivers were distracted, drunk, tired, or simply should not have been driving.  
If that statistic is not sobering enough, consider this. Vehicular accidents are also the number one cause of death for teenagers, again, the majority of which are preventable.  We know from cognitive scientists and psychologists that teenagers are more likely to engage in risky behavior—like dangerous, drunk, or otherwise impaired driving—and, while getting a driver’s license has been a rite-of-passage for a century in America, it’s time to stop the senseless destruction car accidents cause.
Interestingly enough, the Department of Transportation calculated that each person is worth $9.2 million. Like so many things, the government got this one wrong as well. A human life is priceless. Nothing can bring back a loved one who has been killed.
If you have reservations about self-driving cars due of the potential for technological failure, I share them with you. Just this past month, a self-driving car being tested in Arizona turned a corner, struck, and killed a woman who was crossing the street outside of a crosswalk. We have not perfected this technology. We have miles to go before it can be implemented on any kind of large scale. We have many ethical, legal, and policy considerations and implications to work through. However, we have the opportunity to save countless lives. We have the opportunity to save countless others from debilitating injury. There are many advantages for the United States of we were to transition to self-driving cars. The primary benefit is simply the amount of lives saved due to the elimination of intoxicated, distracted, or otherwise dangerous drivers. Computers provide a precise, safe alternative to the potential for human error that occurs each time a driver gets behind the wheel. In addition, self-driving cars can save money on insurance costs and outdated infrastructure, decrease traffic, and are actually better for the environment. However, some limitations to self-driving cars exist. These include a difficult transition to using these vehicles, cost, the failure of technology, and the potential for job loss and decline of certain industries. 
	Despite these technological limitations, an initially high cost, and a likely challenging transition, self-driving cars are the future and they will save lives by eliminating or drastically reducing the possibility of human error. Every year, 33,000 people die in automobile accidents, and most of these deaths could have been prevented because the drivers were distracted, drunk, tired, or otherwise should not have been driving.  In their article, "Top 20 Pros and Cons Associated with Self-Driving Cars," the Auto Insurance Center reports that 81% of automobile accidents are due to human error. Reducing the number of vehicle accidents by 10% would be a huge win. Reducing it by 80% would be astounding. A progression from tens of thousands of traffic fatalities annually due to human error to hypothetically minuscule traffic fatalities due to technological errors alone makes self-driving cars extremely beneficial to society. 
	If that statistic is not sobering enough, consider this. Vehicular accidents are the number one cause of death for teenagers, and again, the majority of these fatalities are preventable. We know from cognitive scientists and psychologists that teenagers are physiologically more likely to engage in risky behavior—such as dangerous, drunk, or otherwise impaired driving— which leads teenagers to take driving risks that adults do not. Unfortunately, we read stories and see horrific pictures in this and other newspapers across the country of teens who have been tragically killed, paralyzed or seriously injured due to car accidents. Each year, high schools around the country ask students to sign the Prom Promise, which is a petition that encourages students not to drink and drive on Prom night. Turning sixteen and getting a driver’s license has long been a rite-of-passage in America. The freedom teens feel when they buy their first car and are able to just go is powerful. Self-driving cars would not strip this freedom and independence from teens--a driver's license and could easily be replaced by a self-driving car operator license--but the self-driving cars would save countless lives while allowing teens their beloved independence and their parents peace of mind. 
	If you have reservations about self-driving cars due of the potential for technological failure, I share them with you. Just this past month, a self-driving car being tested in Arizona turned a corner, struck, and killed a woman who was crossing the street outside of a crosswalk. We have not perfected this technology. We have miles to go before it can be implemented on any kind of large scale. We have many ethical, legal, and policy considerations and implications to work through. However, we have the opportunity to save countless lives. We have the opportunity to save countless others from debilitating injury. We have the obligation to use technology the way it’s always been used—to benefit our society and improve the lives of all Americans.

The advent of self-driving cars presents a transformative opportunity for the United States, promising numerous advantages that could redefine our transportation landscape. Foremost among these is the profound potential to save countless lives by eliminating human error—the root cause of accidents stemming from intoxication, distraction, or other dangerous driving behaviors. Autonomous vehicles offer a precise, consistent, and inherently safer alternative to the inherent fallibility of human drivers. Beyond this paramount benefit, self-driving technology also holds the promise of reducing insurance premiums, modernizing outdated infrastructure, alleviating traffic congestion, and even contributing to a cleaner environment. However, this promising future is not without its challenges, including the complexities of transitioning to new technology, initial costs, the risk of technological failure, and potential disruptions to employment and certain industries.

When considering societal advancements, a fundamental question arises: at what expense to the individual do we support the public good? For self-driving cars, the answer is unequivocally clear. Any innovation with the power to preserve human life constitutes an undeniable benefit, both for the individual and for society as a whole. Despite the recognized technological limitations, initial investment, and a likely challenging transition period, autonomous vehicles represent an inevitable future—one where the primary metric of success is the lives they will save. No other advantage carries such weight; no other limitation should deter us from pursuing this critical evolution.

America currently grapples with a severe and deadly driving problem. Each year, approximately 33,000 individuals tragically lose their lives in automobile accidents, with the vast majority of these deaths being preventable—the result of distracted, impaired, or fatigued drivers. This sobering reality is further compounded by the fact that vehicular accidents remain the leading cause of death for teenagers. Cognitive science confirms adolescents' predisposition to risky behaviors, including dangerous or impaired driving. While acquiring a driver’s license has long been a cherished rite of passage, it is imperative that we confront and halt the senseless destruction these preventable accidents continue to inflict.

Reservations regarding the potential for technological failure are entirely valid, and indeed, widely shared. The recent tragic incident in Arizona, where a self-driving test vehicle struck and killed a pedestrian, starkly reminds us that this technology is not yet perfected. We undoubtedly have significant progress to make, numerous ethical, legal, and policy considerations to address, and rigorous testing ahead before widespread implementation. However, the stakes are too high to falter. We possess the unprecedented opportunity to prevent countless deaths and debilitating injuries. It is not merely an option, but an obligation, to leverage technology as it has always been intended: to advance our society and profoundly improve the lives of all Americans.

Although Back to the Future II predicted cars flying in 2015, the estimate of 2020 for self-driving cars is not that far off. 
	Within the past year, companies like Ford, Honda, BMW and Tesla are some of the automakers that are pushing for self-driving technology. This makes complete sense as technology is increasing forward and will continue to do so as long as there is a need for it. However, there are many drawbacks when implementing something so new and different. There is not a lot of previous data or research to consult with. If an error/accident occurs, it will be difficult to pinpoint the problem. It would be very hard to distinguish if the problem lies with individual car or if it encompasses all self-driving cars. Problems like this will eventually be answered, but it is going to take some time before it can be done efficiently. The advantages outweigh the disadvantages because technology is improving constantly, and there is a need for better transportation. The modes of transportation that are available now work decently, but can always be improved. As a result, society is better off by using self-driving cars because it provides more opportunities and resources that can help improve human life. 
	The best advantage of self-driving cars is to overall reduce human error. Human error is known to be a cause of major accidents, especially when a driver is trying to multi-task and focus on other things besides driving. Although it is easy to forget, the human brain is not a machine. It is an organ that responds uniquely to problems and can produce multiple solutions. A machine differs from the brain because it has a set algorithm programmed to provide an expected solution. There is no wrong solution as the only appropriate solution is already being used. Self-driving cars can help reduce accidents caused by humans since they do not have an inclination to be distracted. The machine has one job and it does it efficiently. As a result, drivers will no longer be distracted when multi-tasking. In addition to reducing human error, self-driving cars will provide more opportunities by providing transportation to those who cannot drive.  
	All in all, technological advances are going to occur as we become smarter as a species. When technology advances too far too quickly, there can be some concerns about the quality of the product and its functionality.
	Potential problems/disadvantages of self-driving cars include an increased rate in unemployment. Driving instructors, DMV tellers and employees of companies like Uber and Lyft, will definitely suffer as there will be less of a demand for those services. Self-driving cars can complete tasks more safely and efficiently than humans. As self-driving cars increase, the amount of jobs available will decrease. Although this is an important concern, technology failure (i.e. glitches), would be an even greater problem. Accidents could be more fatal and insurance rates might increase to account for that. Auto insurance companies already receive way too much money, but if there was a way to get two for the price of one, many people would be more likely to get a self-driving car.

output_text:
The release of *Back to the Future I* and *II* in the 1980s captivated audiences with their visionary depiction of future technology. While significant technological advancements have occurred in recent decades, the widespread reality of flying cars and floating hoverboards remains elusive. Nevertheless, as we approach 2020, the prospect of such innovations no longer seems entirely fantastical. Olivier Garret, writing for Forbes, predicted that 10 million self-driving cars would be on the road by 2020, with autonomous vehicles constituting one out of four cars by 2030. Although *Back to the Future II* ambitiously projected flying vehicles by 2015, Garret's estimate for self-driving cars is remarkably close to the film's prophetic spirit.

In recent years, major automakers such as Ford, Honda, BMW, and Tesla have been at the forefront of developing self-driving technology. This push aligns perfectly with the relentless trajectory of technological progress, which continues to accelerate as long as human needs drive innovation. Given the nascent stage of self-driving cars and the limited comprehensive research available, a thorough understanding of their inherent advantages and disadvantages is crucial before widespread adoption of autonomous vehicles.

The primary benefits of self-driving cars are compelling: they promise a significant reduction in human error by strictly adhering to traffic laws, enable drivers to engage in productive multitasking rather than being distracted, and ultimately foster more efficient transportation systems. As a proponent of technological progress and novel innovations, I believe the advantages of autonomous vehicles generally outweigh their drawbacks. While the rapid pace of technological evolution necessitates that automobiles adapt and keep pace, it is entirely understandable that the public harbors some hesitation, particularly given the substantial concerns that must be addressed prior to full implementation.

However, the integration of autonomous vehicles into society presents several significant challenges. Professions such as driver's education instructors, DMV personnel, and employees of ride-sharing companies like Uber and Lyft are likely to experience a decline in demand, potentially contributing to rising unemployment rates. Even more pressing than job displacement is the concern of technology failure. System glitches could lead to more severe accidents, potentially causing insurance rates to escalate dramatically. It is conceivable that insurance providers might introduce new, ostensibly "necessary" coverage packages specifically for autonomous cars, expanding their revenue streams beyond conventional auto insurance and imposing potentially limitless additional costs on consumers.

In conclusion, self-driving cars are rapidly becoming an integral part of our societal landscape. While this technological advancement offers a multitude of positives alongside notable negatives, the imperative for further comprehensive research and definitive answers remains. Personally, as someone who finds driving tiresome and harbors anxieties about road accidents, I would readily embrace a self-driving car over a traditional vehicle without hesitation.

Self-driving cars are here. They have been piloted in Silicone Valley, San Francisco, New Mexico, Pittsburgh, Moscow, and other places. However, although the technology is here, they are not yet a part of people's lives. The cars are not yet able to drive in all weather conditions or handle anomalous traffic events, like if a storm knocks out traffic lights. Despite these ongoing hurdles self-driving cars have yet to overcome, the training of their neural networks pushes forward because once the technology can be implemented, the cars can solve real-world problems. The cars can make it safe for people with disabilities to run errands without needing to call a friend or a cab. They will reduce the number of traffic fatalities, especially those caused by driver inattention or inebriation. There are plenty of valid concerns about rolling self-driving cars into the market today but tomorrow is another story. Their use will ultimately benefit their passengers and the environment by creating a safer world.

The world is on the brink of environmental disaster. Irreparable damage has already be done, but further harm can be minimized through the advancement of many technologies, self-driving cars are one. Beyond the greater fuel efficiency self-driving cars provide, there is one feature that people do not often consider. Self-driving cars do not need to be owned by an individual. They can be owned by company, such as Zip Car or Uber. Consumers can pay these companies a monthly fee to have a car available to them whenever they need it and they can also opt in to a car pool so that the car picks up others going to the same place they are. Many people drive a car to work and home again, but they do not use the car throughout the day and they certainly don't use it while they are asleep. During times of non-use, the car could be picking up other riders. This would reduce the number of cars on the road, parked on the sides of streets, and adding to the carbon footprint.

There are valid concerns to this. People worry about the loss of jobs associated with self-driving cars as taxi drivers and truck drivers may become a job of the past. However, the job market is constantly evolving. It was not long ago when cars did not exist, ergo these jobs did not exist. The future will bring new fields for people to work in and new streams of revenue. Change is daunting, and many people are nervous about how a world with self-driving cars will function, but there is no question that it will function. People will adapt, as they did to the actual process of driving cars, and the world will be safer for it.
draft3:
Self-driving cars have arrived and are on the market even if their drivers wouldn't define them as such. Drivers are using cars that will auto park, brake if they approach an obstruction, and follow a car in front of them. They may not feel like they are self-driving as they do still require some human interaction and drivers can opt-in to such features, but when they are all used in combination, the car is in effect driving itself. Even when not in control of the wheel, these features can alert drivers to cars in their blind spot and provide a rear view when the driver is in moving backwards. Whether or not people want this future is now irrelevant; it's coming and soon people will be so used to it that they won’t remember what it was like to drive without a little backup support. Eventually, they won’t remember what it was like to drive at all, and that’s a good thing. Everyone will be safer when computers take the wheel.
	People are terrible drivers. We as humans like to believe that we can stay awake, pay attention, and maneuver an enormous hunk of metal at high speeds. The reality is that even when focused on the road, the human brain can only absorb a limited amount of its surroundings. People worry that self-driving cars are not as good as humans at detecting pedestrians or other anomalies, and they aren't as good--they're better at it. Computers have the capability to process the entire environment, not just what's in their fovea or what's left over after the brain has filtered out what it considers irrelevant. And they do it without getting drowsy or lost in their thoughts. Self-driving cars have years of clocked hours on the road. In all of those hours, only one pedestrian fatality has been caused by a self-driving car in the United States. On the other hand, 23 cyclists were killed in 2017 by human-driven cars in New York City alone (CBS New York). Clearly self-driving cars already have a better track record than their predecessors. 
	However, people should not be surrendering unmonitored control of the driving to their car just yet. There are some edges to human cognition that self-driving cars do not currently share even if they surpass it on others. One of them is the knowledge that some caution should be taken when accelerating around turns or at the peak of hills. I have been in cars that are set to regulate their speed to conditions of the road and the drivers around them, but on a nice day with no other cars in sight of the cameras, they will ramp up speed as they reach the peak of the hill without any knowledge of what may be on the other side. Due to the superior reaction times of a computer as compared to a human, the car could likely avoid any unforeseen obstruction that was just at the top and out of sight of the hill, but its passengers would not have enjoyed the abrupt stop. Even without an accident, such an abrupt stop can cause air bags to deploy or cause the body to jerk against the seatbelt and snap the collar bone. Such a need for abrupt stops would have been eliminated by a careful human driver who slowed down in anticipation of what might be lurking just beyond the crest of the hill. 
	The fact that self-driving cars are not ready to chauffeur people around en masse yet does not mean that it’s not coming though or that this reality is anything to fear. These cars just need more training, and unlike people, when one car trains, they all learn. Whether its navigating the Pittsburgh left or learning to be cautious when its camera does not detect straight road ahead, when one car learns, they all learn because they all share the technology. Thankfully, for the consumer, the transition to a self-driving car is a slow one. People will have time to adjust to the new reality and the software engineers will use this time to improve the technology. We have moved from basic cruise control to cars that will match pace with traffic. We have moved from rear-view cameras to cars that parallel park themselves. People are feeling safer about letting their car take over and when consumers are ready to give up the wheel, the car manufacturers will be there with a self-driving version. When that day comes, we’ll all be safer for it, and maybe the number of cyclists killed in New York City in a given year will finally reach zero. 
	CBSNewYork. “NYC Pedestrian Deaths Drop 32 Percent Last Year.” CBS New York, CBS New York, 8 Jan. 2018, newyork.cbslocal.com/2018/01/08/2017-pedestrian-deaths-nyc/.

Autonomous vehicles are no longer a futuristic dream; they are a present reality, having undergone pilot programs in diverse locations such as Silicon Valley, San Francisco, New Mexico, Pittsburgh, and Moscow. While the underlying technology exists, these vehicles have yet to fully integrate into daily life. Current limitations include navigating extreme weather and handling unforeseen traffic anomalies, like power outages affecting signal lights. Despite these developmental hurdles, the relentless refinement of their neural networks continues, driven by the immense potential for self-driving cars to address critical real-world challenges. They promise enhanced mobility for individuals with disabilities, offering independence without reliance on friends or taxis. Crucially, they are poised to significantly reduce traffic fatalities, particularly those stemming from human error, distraction, or impairment. Although valid concerns about immediate mass deployment persist, the long-term vision positions autonomous vehicles as a transformative force, ultimately benefiting both passengers and the environment by fostering a safer world.

The looming threat of environmental disaster necessitates urgent technological innovation to mitigate further harm, and autonomous vehicles present a significant part of this solution. Beyond their inherent fuel efficiency, self-driving cars offer a less obvious, yet profound, environmental advantage: they challenge traditional individual car ownership. Instead, these vehicles can operate as part of a shared fleet managed by companies akin to Zip Car or Uber. Consumers could subscribe to a service, gaining on-demand access to a vehicle and the option to carpool with others heading in the same direction. Consider that most personal cars sit idle for the majority of the day and night. During these periods of non-use, an autonomous vehicle could be actively serving other riders. This paradigm shift would drastically reduce the overall number of vehicles on roads, alleviate parking congestion, and substantially diminish our collective carbon footprint.

Naturally, the advent of autonomous technology raises legitimate concerns, particularly regarding potential job displacement for professions like taxi and truck drivers. However, the labor market has always been in a state of flux. It is worth remembering that the very concept of these driving jobs emerged only with the invention of the automobile itself. The future promises new industries and novel revenue streams, creating roles we cannot yet imagine. While societal transformation can be unsettling, prompting anxiety about how a world with self-driving cars will operate, there is little doubt it *will* function. Humanity has consistently adapted to profound technological shifts, just as it did to the initial widespread adoption of cars. Ultimately, this adaptation will lead to a safer and more efficient world.

The 21st century has witnessed an unparalleled surge in technological advancement, with humans leveraging high computing power and sophisticated algorithms to automate an increasing array of tasks. While driving remains a passion for some, it has become a mundane necessity for many. Although improvements in road infrastructure have somewhat eased the effort, the promise of self-driving cars to eliminate this daily chore is compelling. Such technology could significantly reduce the burden on professional drivers, for whom driving is a job rather than a choice. While I strongly advocate for continued research in this field, I harbor significant reservations about the widespread implementation of self-driving vehicles.

For many, particularly millennials, the ability to drive became accessible at an early age, facilitated by a growing global economy and more affordable vehicles. While self-driving cars could undoubtedly reduce the cognitive load for some, they may prove redundant or even problematic for others. Having grown up in the foothills of the Himalayas, I experienced firsthand the challenges of driving on treacherous roads with limited infrastructure. Navigating deadly Himalayan curves demands unwavering concentration, a challenge that will persist in such remote areas. Self-driving cars have demonstrated exceptional performance in controlled environments like the United States, a nation celebrated for its advanced infrastructure. However, the success of these vehicles currently relies heavily on highly accurate GPS mapping systems, a level of precision not universally available. Furthermore, GPS reliability can be compromised by adverse weather conditions, directly impacting the safety of autonomous vehicles. The underlying machine learning algorithms, while sophisticated, cannot yet guarantee 100% accuracy, raising serious concerns in unforeseen scenarios.

Another significant hurdle is the human element. Many individuals, especially older generations, remain reluctant to fully embrace advanced technology or surrender control to a machine. This hesitation and potential distrust could lead to critical malfunctions or dangerous situations if drivers are unable or unwilling to intervene effectively. Beyond human acceptance, the financial implications of large-scale implementation are immense. The cost of developing, deploying, and maintaining such systems, coupled with the necessary upgrades to road infrastructure, far surpasses the projected utility of a complete autonomous vehicle program.

The touted reduction in accidents, a primary justification for self-driving cars, has recently faced skepticism. Numerous incidents involving autonomous vehicles have demonstrated that they do not entirely eliminate the risk of accidents. A simple system malfunction can lead to severe consequences, and a human operator, disengaged from active driving, might struggle to comprehend and react to an issue within the critical seconds available. On challenging terrains like Himalayan roads, where constant vigilance is paramount, a moment's malfunction in a self-driving car could prove deadly.

In conclusion, while the concept of self-driving vehicles is innovative and holds great promise, its broad implementation is constrained by multiple factors. These include civil infrastructure limitations, GPS availability and reliability, adverse weather conditions, and prohibitive financial costs. Therefore, while autonomous technology may find feasible applications in specific, controlled environments where these constraints can be met, its widespread deployment on a grand scale remains a premature and unfeasible endeavor.

The article "Top 20 Pros and Cons Associated With Self-Driving Cars", provides an in depth look into the reasoning of whether or not we, as a society, should adopt self-driving vehicles.  Of the 20 "pros" listed, an increase in safety was likely the leading factor, but also included were the benefits in commute times and city travel, the possibility for the driver to do something productive in the time that would otherwise be used for driving, and the idea that elderly or disabled people would be able to ride self-driving cars without worry.  However, in contrast to the advantages listed in the article, the author also offered 20 disadvantages.  Many of these focused on the training involved in learning how to operate it, the high cost, security issues (both personal and cybersecurity), legal ramifications, the fact that a self-driving vehicle will not be able to appropriately respond to all situations and other technical hangups,and the financial implications caused by uprooting an old industry. 

When weighing the "pros" and "cons" in order to gauge whether adopting self-driving cars would be beneficial to society, it is important to look at the whole picture.  The "pros" are all valid, while it appears that many of the "cons" can be addressed as the self-driving vehicles further develop.  For example, the high initial cost is cited as a reason against adopting these vehicles.  However, what isn't taken into consideration in this case is the fact that new technologies typically drastically drop in price over a short amount of time.  We often hear the story of the first computers being the size of an entire room and costing hundreds of thousands of dollars at the time.  However, in a few tens of years the price of those computers came down enough that many families could afford them.  I believe a similar situation could occur with the sensor and computer package required in self-driving vehicles.  As a second example, cybersecurity has become a very hot topic recently and appears in the "cons" against these vehicles.  However, several very critical pieces of infrastructure are operated and they have been largely protected against cybersecurity attacks.  Take for example remote controlled drones currently in use by the military.  The risk of a cyber attack is definitely present, but the designers have found a way to adequately protect the devices.  Something similar could be done for self-driving vehicles.

Although several more examples supporting the idea that many of the present "cons" seen in self-driving vehicle adoption can be corrected in the future, it is unnecessary seeing that they are quite similar.  However, what is important to notice is that all of the "pros" do not seem as if they will be changing, even as the vehicles themselves improve.  Therefore I recommend the gradual adoption of self-driving vehicles into society.

The article "Top 20 Pros and Cons Associated With Self-Driving Cars" thoroughly examines the societal implications of adopting autonomous vehicles. Among the two dozen advantages highlighted, enhanced safety emerged as a primary driver. Other significant benefits included optimized commute times and urban mobility, the opportunity for occupants to engage in productive activities during travel, and increased accessibility for the elderly and disabled. Conversely, the author enumerated twenty disadvantages. These concerns spanned operational training, prohibitive costs, multifaceted security issues (encompassing both personal data and cybersecurity), complex legal ramifications, inherent limitations in a vehicle's ability to adapt to all unforeseen situations, and the economic disruption stemming from industry upheaval.

To accurately assess the societal benefits of autonomous vehicle adoption, a comprehensive evaluation of these pros and cons is essential. While the listed advantages are inherently compelling and enduring, many of the disadvantages appear to be surmountable challenges that will diminish with technological maturity. Consider, for instance, the concern regarding high initial costs. This perspective often overlooks the historical trend of new technologies experiencing significant price reductions shortly after market introduction. Early computers, for example, once occupied entire rooms and cost hundreds of thousands of dollars. Yet, within a few decades, their prices plummeted, making them affordable for many families. A similar trajectory is highly probable for the advanced sensor and computing packages integral to autonomous vehicles. Similarly, cybersecurity, a prominent concern for autonomous vehicles, is a challenge that has been successfully addressed in other critical infrastructure and sensitive technologies. While the risk of a cyber attack is undeniably present, as demonstrated by remote-controlled drones currently in military use, designers have consistently found ways to implement robust protective measures. Comparable, highly secure solutions can undoubtedly be developed and integrated into self-driving vehicles, mitigating this perceived disadvantage.

While numerous other examples could illustrate how current disadvantages of autonomous vehicles are likely to be resolved with future advancements, the recurring nature of these solutions renders further elaboration unnecessary. Crucially, the inherent benefits—the 'pros'—are unlikely to diminish; rather, they stand to be amplified as the technology matures. Consequently, a phased integration of autonomous vehicles into society is warranted.

output_text:
Autonomous vehicles, once a futuristic concept, are rapidly becoming a reality, sparking considerable debate about their societal impact. While the transition to self-driving cars presents both compelling advantages and significant challenges, a close examination reveals that their potential benefits, particularly in safety and efficiency, position them as a transformative technology for the better.

One of the most compelling arguments for self-driving cars is their potential to drastically improve road safety. Human error accounts for the vast majority of accidents, and autonomous systems promise a significant reduction in car crashes. The U.S. Department of Transportation values a human life at over $9 million, translating into significant cost savings in insurance and healthcare associated with accident recovery. Beyond safety, these vehicles offer unparalleled convenience, allowing occupants to utilize commute time for other tasks. They are also expected to optimize traffic flow, reduce congestion, decrease commute times, and conserve fuel, benefiting both individuals and the environment. Furthermore, widespread adoption could enhance transportation accessibility in larger cities and potentially lead to reduced taxes by eliminating the need for driver's license issuance.

Despite these advantages, the widespread adoption of self-driving cars faces substantial hurdles. The initial cost of these advanced vehicles will likely be prohibitive for low-income individuals, creating a potential accessibility gap. A gradual, incomplete transition to autonomous vehicles means that the full benefits of safety and efficiency would not be realized, as accidents and inefficiencies would persist among mixed traffic. Economically, the transportation sector faces significant disruption, with potential job losses for freight drivers and taxi operators, which could negatively impact unemployment rates and the broader economy. Practical limitations also exist, such as the vulnerability of sophisticated sensors to severe weather conditions like heavy rain, which could compromise vehicle performance and safety. Finally, there's a concern that drivers might lose the necessary skills to operate a vehicle manually, creating a dependence on autonomous systems.

However, the rapid advancements in autonomous technology, marked by improved mechanics, accuracy, and algorithms since their early development, strongly tip the scales towards their advantages. The most powerful argument for self-driving cars remains their capacity to save lives. Research by McKinsey & Company in 2015 projected a 90% reduction in traffic fatalities once autonomous vehicles are fully deployed. Given that over 37,000 people died in motor vehicle crashes in 2016, according to the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration, autonomous vehicles present the most promising solution to this leading cause of death. Beyond direct safety, self-driving cars could also significantly de-escalate interactions with law enforcement. By adhering strictly to traffic laws, they could reduce instances of traffic stops, thereby mitigating the risk of confrontations that can tragically escalate into police brutality, shootings, or unwarranted arrests.

In conclusion, while the path to a fully autonomous future is fraught with economic, social, and technical challenges, the overwhelming potential for self-driving cars to enhance safety, improve efficiency, and positively transform daily life is undeniable. The evolution of this technology has moved it from a speculative concept to a tangible solution for some of society's most pressing transportation issues. Embracing autonomous vehicles is not merely an acceptance of new technology, but a commitment to a future with safer roads and more equitable, efficient transportation systems.

The new technology of self-driving cars is decades in the making in Pittsburgh, with Carnegie Mellon first leading the way in the 1990s.  Since then the city has been a literal testing ground for companies like Uber and Argo AI, with the mayor keen on attracting (and keeping) talent at the forefront of this technology.  Reactions have long been mixed, though, even before a fatal accident in Arizona recently halted Uber automated test drives.  The strong opposition to automated vehicles in the aftermath, however, is somewhat surprising, given how much safer the technology tends to be.  Or, rather, how unsafe human drivers actually are.

A single death at the hands of an automated Uber stopped its vehicle testing, yet eighty-one percent of vehicular accidents are the result of human error.  Those in working in Oakland routinely see that this human error extends to pedestrians and cyclists in addition to human drivers.  Humans lack the reaction time to respond to unexpected changes in scenery, from a pedestrian stepping out onto Forbes to a car swerving abruptly to miss a pothole.  Human drivers are also increasingly distracted, and are choosing to distract themselves with a dizzying array of activities from personal grooming to streaming television.  Self-driving cars also solve the problem of drunk driving, as autonomous vehicles double as designated drivers.  In a city with limited taxi service and practically no commercial designated driving companies, drunk drivers find themselves tempted to drive from the Southside to the South Hills with often destructive consequences.

Accessibility and inclusion are on residents' minds, too, as the city plans to implement a rapid transit system that will make current connections faster but will leave lower income and disabled locals out of the discussion.  Fleets of self-driving cars would not only ferry drunk passengers but others without access to vehicles as well, including those in neighborhoods lacking in public transportation.  Pittsburgh's elderly and disabled, who used to be better served by paratransit services before budget cuts gutted these systems, are especially vulnerable by their lack of access.

The counter to the accessibility argument is, of course, the high cost of self-driving vehicles.  With an initial cost of more than $100,000, there is little overlap between the people who currently rely on public transportation and those who can immediately purchase self-driving vehicles.  Not many car owners could afford a $100,000 vehicle, for that matter.  This is alleviated by two considerations, though.  The cost of this technology will inevitably go down, just like the brand new CDs, Nintendos, and personal computers of our youth.  What is unaffordable now may not be in a few years.  What is less certain, though, is whether the city can shed its car-first mentality and embrace shared self-driving vehicles, a public personal transit system.

Since then, the city has been a literal testing ground for companies like Uber and Argo AI, with Mayor Peduto keen on attracting (and keeping) the talent at the forefront of this technology. Car makers have been introducing automated technology in baby steps, from rear-facing cameras to park-assist features and obstacle sensors.  Reactions have long been mixed, though, even before a fatal accident in Arizona recently halted Uber automated test drives.  What is surprising is the strong opposition to automated vehicles in the aftermath, given how much safer the technology tends to be. Or, rather, how unsafe human drivers actually are. This danger is not just limited to drivers, but to everyone in our neighborhoods: pedestrians, cyclists, and bus-riders alike. The increased safety of our roads for all residents that we will see with self-driving cars is the greatest reason to advocate for their use throughout the city. 
	A single death at the hands of an automated Uber stopped its vehicle testing, yet the Center for Disease Control estimates over 33,000 Americans are killed each year by automobiles.  An alarming  81% of these vehicular accidents are the result of human error. Those working in Oakland routinely see that this human error extends to pedestrians and cyclists in addition to human drivers. Humans lack the reaction time to respond to unexpected changes in scenery, from a pedestrian stepping out onto Forbes to a car swerving abruptly to miss a pothole. Human drivers are also increasingly distracted, and are choosing to distract themselves with an obscene array of activities from personal grooming to streaming television. For those distracted and inhibited by alcohol, self-driving cars also solve the problem of drunk driving, as autonomous vehicles double as designated drivers. In a city with limited taxi service and practically no private designated driving companies, drunk drivers find themselves tempted to make the drive from the Southside to the South Hills, with often destructive consequences. 
	Accessibility and inclusion are on residents' minds, too, as the city plans to implement a rapid transit system that will make current connections faster but will leave lower-income and disabled locals on the curb. Fleets of self-driving cars would not only ferry drunk passengers but others without access to vehicles as well, including those in neighborhoods lacking in public transportation. Pittsburgh's elderly and disabled, who used to be better served by paratransit services before budget cuts gutted these systems, are especially vulnerable by their lack of access. 
	The counter to the accessibility argument is, of course, the high cost of self-driving vehicles. With an initial outlay of more than $100,000, there is little overlap between the people who currently rely on public transportation and those who can immediately purchase self-driving vehicles. Not many car owners could afford a $100,000 vehicle, for that matter. This is alleviated by two considerations, though. The cost of this technology will inevitably go down, just like the brand new CDs, Nintendos, and personal computers of our youth. What is unaffordable now may not be in a few years. What is less certain, though, is whether the city can shed its car-first mentality and embrace shared self-driving vehicles, a public personal transit system.  Uber and Lyft have revolutionized Pittsburgh's taxi scene in just a few years, after all, and those improvements to accessibility and availability will improve as the numbers of self-driving cars increase.
	Few weeks go by where out city is not making national headlines for its livability, its technology, and its overall rust-belt renaissance. Self-driving cars are currently at the vanguard of this movement, and both attract talent to the region and retain the minds trained by Carnegie Mellon and Pitt. While this has a cachet of modernity and elitism, the benefits of self-driving cars reach everyone. Indeed, pockets of our city now under served and under represented will better enjoy the benefits of our "rust belt renaissance."  When people really look at the costs of human drivers---and human errors---the choice to adapt self-driving cars throughout the city will be an easy one.

It seems that cars have always been the same: four wheels, a body, a steering wheel. They are ridiculously simple! Compare to the history of bicycles for example. If you happen to have seen those old black and white photographs of early bicycles, you will see a complex evolution, akin to those in biology! Nevertheless, something on the horizon is going to turn everything about cars upside down (well, the cars themselves will probably still remain on their four wheels, though!)

Self-driving cars are already among us fueling the debate over their good and and evil. Proponents of automated cars argue that self-driving cars will eliminate the catastrophic number of fatal car crashes because the cold-blooded machines will not be distracted by music, a passer-by, or be under the influence of alcohol and drugs. The other feature they cite is the possibility for many, or even all, cars in a certain city to communicate with one another to coordinate the speed levels, and red light waits so as to achieve the maximum flow and minimum travel times. Automated cars, they say, will allow for more cars to drive next to each other, due to their precision in maneuvering, and yet lead to less congestion. Another major advantage will be their service to the disabled and elderly people. Once a good proportion of cars on the street become self-drivers, the advocates claim that the whole society will benefit: saving lives, avoiding financial setbacks from car accidents, less fuel consumption, fewer traffic police officers on duty, etc. 

However, the opponents of the technology have some legitimate concerns. In this category there are some superficial and trivial arguments that can be refuted right away. For example, the phobia of a scenario where a car breaks down and the human driver, having forgotten how to drive, has no idea of what to do, leading to chaos in the city cannot be a real argument against self-driving cars. Already today, an average individual has no skill to get a car to start up if it all of a sudden stops in the middle of street. Such a situation rarely disrupts a whole city! Some other arguments question the limits of the technology. What if it snows? What if the road is full of potholes (such as those in Pittsburgh!)? Well, these while valid concerns do not prove the technology insufficient. There are improvements coming every day and all those conditions will be taken care of.

With the obvious criticism of the self-driving cars out of way, we arrive at some deep issues against the technology. On the infrastructure level, there is the problem of providing cyber security to such a delicate network of interconnected software that store and communicate data between cars. If, as Guardian suggests, there is a potential for hackers to manipulate such a system, then it will be a true disaster. Achieving a high level of security while possible could be prohibitively costly, overshadowing any amount of saving on gasoline.

However, the biggest challenge facing self-driving cars is their popularity. The pros listed above all are predicated on the hope that they will come in large quantities. The effect of having cars coordinate their paths will not be felt if they substitute only 2% of all the cars out there. Same holds true for safety. Have you noticed that sometimes, ironically, the safest drivers, are the very cause of some accidents? Imagine a road where the speed limit is 50, but "everyone knows that it is ok to go 60 (when the police is not around!)". Then that one guy who sticks to that 50 mph limit is the source of frustration for those behind him/her and a possible danger for everyone involved. Thus, the advantages of autonomous cars will begin to take effect only after a good portion of the society adopts them.

But with the currently high cost of these cars, and the natural public resistance to change, it seems that self-driving cars will, for the coming years, live in the margins, only triggering imagination and debates from time to time. At this point in time, we are just not ready yet to embrace them -- definitely not in large numbers.
